channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: One in five trying to quit vaping used cigarettes, survey finds
item.description: A survey by Action on Smoking and Health reveals that 19% of adults attempting to quit vaping used cigarettes, potentially due to misconceptions about the risks. Beliefs about the harm of smoking versus vaping influenced these choices. While vaping has aided 3.3 million former smokers and daily vaping has surpassed smoking in Great Britain, misconceptions persist with 52% of current smokers believing vaping is as harmful as smoking. The UK government is considering further regulations on vaping to prevent youth uptake. Cancer Research UK supports regulations that deter non-smokers while recognizing vaping's harm reduction for smokers. Balancing visibility while highlighting differences between vaping and smoking is crucial to prevent setbacks in smoking cessation efforts. (Published on July 15, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)

item.title: Disposables Banned, Flavors Next: Irish Vape Crackdown Gathers Pace
item.description: Remove the products adults actually use to stay off cigarettes, and the outcome may not be the one policymakers expect. Ireland’s proposed restrictions on disposable vapes and flavours risk making vaping less accessible, less appealing and less useful for people trying to quit smoking. Protecting young people matters, but so does avoiding a policy that pushes adults back towards cigarettes or into informal markets. Good regulation should reduce harm in practice, not just look tough on paper. (Published on July 15, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: Tobacco Harm Reduction for Older Smokers
item.description: Tobacco harm reduction could benefit older adults struggling to quit smoking, as they often face deep nicotine dependence. Traditional cessation methods are often ineffective for long-term smokers. Tobacco harm reduction offers diverse alternatives, like e-cigarettes and nicotine pouches, that better address nicotine dependence. Research shows older smokers may lack knowledge about these alternatives. Encouraging accurate information and exploring harm reduction options can support older adults in making the switch and overcoming barriers to quitting. Involving their community can provide essential support. Embracing harm reduction offers a realistic path forward for older smokers, even after years of tobacco use. (Published on July 15, 2026bytobaccoharmreduction.net)
